coredata.dc:title: An efficient solid-state synthesis of fluorescent surface carboxylated carbon dots derived from C60 as a label-free probe for iron ions in living cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lan, Jing
Liu, Chunfang
Gao, Mingxuan
Huang, Chengzhi
coredata.dc:description: In order to achieve the simple, easily repeated, and large scale preparation of fluorescent CDs, a new solid-state synthesis (SSS) approach was developed by calcining the mixture of fullerenes (C60) and solid sodium hydroxide. The cage of fullerenes could be opened and the hydroxyl and carboxyl were successfully introduced in the presence of sodium hydroxide under high temperature. The as-prepared surface carboxylated CDs possess many good properties, such as high water solubility, good photostability, salt tolerance, and nontoxicity. Especially, the fluorescence of CDs could be highly quenched by Fe3+ because of the strong interaction of hydroxyl or carboxyl on the as-obtained CDs with Fe3+, which realized a sensitive detection of Fe3+ in the linear range of 0.02–0.6μmol/L. What is more, we further applied the obtained CDs into the intracellular imaging of Fe3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Carboxylation
Solid-state synthesis (SSS)
Intracellular imaging
Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphologic characterization of the CDs. (A) TEM image of the CDs, Inset: particle size distribution of CDs. (B) AFM image of the CDs deposited on freshly cleaved mica substrates.

Optical properties of the CDs. (A) FTIR spectra of CDs, (B) XPS high resolutions scan of C1s region of CDs, and (C) UV–vis absorption (ABS), PLE (with the emission wavelength of 410nm) and PL (at 290nm excitation) spectra of the CDs dispersed in water, Inset: photograph of the CDs aqueous solution taken under visible light (left) and 365nm ultraviolet lamp (right), respectively. (D) PL spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelength, Inset: normalized PL spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelength.

The application of CDs for the detection of Fe3+. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution with different concentration of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.02, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, and 8μmol/L), Inset: the dependence of 1−I/I 0 on the concentration of Fe3+ within the range of 0.02–0.6μmol/L; (B) Effects of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. (C) Imaging of Fe(III) in A549 cells. (A, E) The fluorescence images (A) and overlay of bright-field and fluorescence images (E) of A549 cells. (B, F) The fluorescence images (B) and overlay of bright-field and fluorescence images (F) of A549 cells incubated with 40μg/mL CDs for 24h at 37°C and 5% CO2. (C, D, G, and H) Fluorescence images and overlay of bright-field and fluorescence images of A549 cells for the identification of Fe3+. The A549 cells were first incubated with CDs for 24h, after washing with PBS three times, the cells were incubated with (C, G) 0.02mmol/L Fe3+, (G, H) 0.1mmol/L Fe3+ for 1h, respectively.
Illustration of the formation procedure and application of CDs.

head.ce:title: An efficient solid-state synthesis of fluorescent surface carboxylated carbon dots derived from C60 as a label-free probe for iron ions in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400716, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to achieve the simple, easily repeated, and large scale preparation of fluorescent CDs, a new solid-state synthesis (SSS) approach was developed by calcining the mixture of fullerenes (C60) and solid sodium hydroxide. The cage of fullerenes could be opened and the hydroxyl and carboxyl were successfully introduced in the presence of sodium hydroxide under high temperature. The as-prepared surface carboxylated CDs possess many good properties, such as high water solubility, good photostability, salt tolerance, and nontoxicity. Especially, the fluorescence of CDs could be highly quenched by Fe3+ because of the strong interaction of hydroxyl or carboxyl on the as-obtained CDs with Fe3+, which realized a sensitive detection of Fe3+ in the linear range of 0.02–0.6μmol/L. What is more, we further applied the obtained CDs into the intracellular imaging of Fe3+.

By calcining the mixture of fullerenes (C60) and solid sodium hydroxide, surface carboxylated CDs were prepared, which possess the properties of high water solubility, good photostability, salt tolerance, nontoxicity, and could be highly selectively quenched by Fe3+ because of the strong interaction of hydroxyl or carboxyl on the as-obtained CDs with Fe3+, realizing the intracellular imaging of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted particular attention owing to their good properties (i.e. excellent photoluminescence (PL), low cytotoxicity, photostability, and biocompatability) [1–3] and have been applied into various fields including biosensing [4–6] and bioimaging [7–9] since the first discovery in 2004 by Xu et al. [10]. To date, CDs with various sizes and optical properties have been successfully prepared through two strategies, namely “top-down” and “bottom-up”. For the former strategy, chunks of larger carbon structures are cut into nanoscale CDs with the assistance of physical or chemical methods, including discharge [10], laser ablation [3–11], and electrochemical oxidation [12,13]. The latter strategy mainly consists of hydrothermal synthesis [2], microwave synthesis [14], and supported synthetic [15], in which the CDs are derived from molecule precursors. Typically, the preparation of CDs was mainly carried on in the solutions regardless of top-down or bottom-up. Although these synthesis strategies could successfully yield fluorescent and aqueous dispersed CDs, it is obvious that complex and time-consuming synthesis, severe synthetic conditions, as well as low yield are still remained to be resolved.

Solid-state synthesis (SSS) has widely been an effective approach for the fabrication of low-dimension nanostructures [16–18]. Generally, it starts from the core formed in certain site, and reaction dynamics depends on the formation and growth speed of the core. The core will quickly grow once it is formed because the formation activation energy is higher than growth activation energy [19,20]. Compared with other synthesis approaches, mainly including gas-state synthesis and liquid-state synthesis, SSS strategy has many advantages, including simple operation, solvent-free for the mass-production, in line with the concept of green chemistry [21], low cost, and being able to industrialization. Up to now, many researches have been carried out using solid-state synthesis to prepare fluorescent nanoparticles. For example, Lu et al. have successfully synthesized fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on a ruthenium surface by using C60 molecules as a precursor, in which the cage of C60 was opened by the metal catalyzed [22]. Chen’s group prepared an N-doped fluorescent CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield by using PEG-diamine and citric acid as nitrogen and carbon source via solid-phase synthesis [23]. Herein, we reported a novel SSS method to prepare fluorescent surface carboxylated CDs by directly calcining the mixture of C60 and solid sodium hydroxide at a high temperature. Compared with Chen’s work, we only chose C60 as carbon source, which had no any functional groups on their surface, to synthesize fluorescent CDs and successfully realized the surface carboxylation of CDs through dry heat treatment in the presence of NaOH rather than other materials with functional groups, which provided a novel strategy for the surface functionalization of CDs.

Ferric ions (Fe3+) are an important transition metal in cellular systems, which could combine with various regulatory proteins, especially hemoglobin [24], and take part in many physiological activities, including the enzyme synthesis, energy metabolism and hematopoietic function, and immunologic function. However, overdose or deficiency of Fe3+ would result in physiological function disorder or disease [25–27]. To date, various methods have been developed for the Fe3+ detection, such as atomic absorption/emission spectrophotometry [28], atomic fluorescence and UV spectrometry [29], etc. Although these methods could achieve the accurate quantitative of Fe3+, sophisticated instrumentation and complex preparation were required, which limited the widely application of these methods. On the contrary, the methods based on fluorescent nanomaterials fulfill the simple, rapid, and cheap detection of Fe3+. In this work, we displayed a new concept for Fe3+-sensitive detection based on the fluorescence quenching of CDs, in which only fluorescent CDs as label-free probe were needed in whole procedure. What is more, we further applied the as-prepared CDs into the intracellular imaging of Fe3+.

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Fullerene was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide was received from Chuandong Chemical Group Co., Ltd. The stock solutions of metal ions were all prepared from their chloride salts to 10−3 mol/L. Millipore purified water (18.2MΩ) was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a JEM-2100 microscopy (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained from a Vecco multimode Nanoscope TM scanning probe microsystem (Vecco, USA) with TESP7 tips. A FTIR-8400S Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to record the IR spectra. An ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was used for recording XPS C 1s spectra. Raman spectrum was taken using a LabRAM HR 800 Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France). Absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured at room temperature with a 3600 UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a 2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were recorded with a FL-TCSPC fluorescent spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France). An Olympus E-510 digital camera was used to capture the photographs of solutions. The cell viability was measured by Mode 680 Microplate Reader (BIO-RAD, USA). Fluorescence imaging of living cells was taken on Olympus IX-81 Inverted Fluorescence Microscope (Olympus, Japan).

Scheme 1 displayed the synthesis procedure of fluorescent surface functionalized CDs. In a typical procedure, 10mg C60 and 250mg NaOH were first ground evenly before transformed into a ceramic crucible, and then the crucible was calcined in the muffle furnace for 4h at 300°C. After cooled down, a brown product was obtained. The product was dissolved in water and then centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min. The upper brown solution was collected and then dialyzed against ultrapure water in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for 24h. Pure CDs powder was obtained by drying the dialyzed solution through freeze-drying.

The living human lung cancer cells A549 were grown in MEM (modified Eagle’s medium) supplemented with 10% FBS (fetal bovine serum) at 37°C and 5% CO2. The CKK-8 assays were carried out through seeding A549 cells in a 96-well plate at a relative even density for every well, and incubated overnight. The following day, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) for once, followed by adding 90μL culture medium and 10μL PBS (as control) or 10μL different concentrations of CDs. After incubated for another 24h, the PBS was used to wash the cells for once, and then added 90μL culture medium with 2% FBS and 10μL CKK-8 reagent. The absorbance of each well was recorded at 450nm by the Mode 680 Microplate Reader following incubated them 1h and the cell viability was calculated according to the absorbance.

Before the fluorescence imaging experiments, the A549 cells were transferred and seeded on a 24-well plate with clean slides in culture medium incubating for 24h at 37°C, 5% CO2. Then, the cells were washed with PBS three times, followed by incubating with 40μg/mL CDs for another 24h, and washed with PBS three times before incubating with 4% paraformaldehyde about half an hour at 4°C for fixing cells. And then, the slide with cells on it was transferred to another clean glass slide after the cells were washed with water twice to remove redundant paraformaldehyde, besides, the slide with cells was fixed by glycerol. Experiments to detect Fe3+ in living cells were accomplished by incubating with 0.02mmol/L or 0.1mmol/L Fe3+ for 1h. All the concentrations mentioned here are the final values. Cell imaging was carried out on the Olympus IX-81 inverted fluorescence microscope after the glycerol dried.

The morphology of the as-prepared CDs was identified by transmission electron microscope (TEM) ( Fig. 1A) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 1B). The results showed that the as-prepared CDs were mono-dispersed and nanospherical. The average size of CDs was 2.03±0.91nm with a lattice spacing of 0.33nm (Upper right inset of Fig. 1A). From the histogram (Lower left inset of Fig. 1A), we could find that the distribution of CDs was narrow from 1.5nm to 3.5nm. What is more, the height of CDs was around 2.5±1.5nm, which was close to the diameter of CDs.

To verify the surface chemistry of the as-prepared CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) spectrum were characterized in this study. The absorption band at 3444cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH. The appearance of absorption bands at 2931cm−1 and 2858cm−1 ascribed to the –C–H stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2, respectively, suggested the occurrence of sp3 hybridization oxidized from sp2 hybrid carbons in C60. The absorption band at 1022cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–O, 1600cm−1 to 1700cm−1 was corresponding to C=O stretching vibrations [30]. All of the above results demonstrated the successful introduction of hydroxyls and carboxyl/carboxylate groups on the surface of CDs. The XPS spectra shown in Fig. 2B also confirmed the presence of hydroxyls and carboxyl/carboxylate groups. The binding energies of 284.6, 285.9 and 289.1eV were attributed to the C=C, C–OH/C–O–C and –COOH, respectively, which implied that the as-prepared CDs were mainly composed of sp2 carbons with some sp3 carbon defects and there were abundant hydroxyls and carboxyl/carboxylate groups on their surfaces. The introduction of hydroxyls and carboxyl/carboxylate groups was resulted from the oxidation of the sp2 structure of C60 in the presence of NaOH and high temperature. What is more, the existence of an apparent narrow G band at 1590cm−1 and a weak broad D band at 1360cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of the CDs confirmed the partially broken structure of C60 during the calcination process (Fig. S1, ESI†).

The optical properties of CDs were characterized by the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The CDs showed a band absorbance from 200nm to 600nm with a broad shoulder peak around at 260nm, which indicated a broken symmetry transition of C60 [31] and the formation of n→π* transitions from C=O [32]. A well-defined emission peak was found at 400nm when excited with 290nm. The color of CDs solution was brown and blue under daylight and 365nm UV light lamp, respectively. What is more, the emission peak of CDs red shifted from 400 to 510nm with the increase of excitation wavelength from 290nm to 440nm, which was probably resulted from the different particle size and/or the considerable distribution of emissive trap sites on the surface of CDs. In addition, the quantum yield of CDs was calculated as 4.82% by using quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. S2, ESI†) and a typical fluorescence life time of CDs was measured as 7.8ns (Table 1, Fig. S3, ESI†), indicating the radiation recombination nature of excitons [14–33].

To further explore the application of the as-prepared CDs, we evaluated the stability of CDs under some conditions such as pH, ion strength, and continuous illumination. As shown in the supporting information (Fig. S4A, ESI†), neither the peak position nor the fluorescence intensity of the CDs changed even the concentration of NaCl was up to 3.0mol/L. Meanwhile, the CDs showed a good photostability under continuous excitation for 30min (Fig. S4B, ESI†). However, we found that the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the CDs increased gradually as the value of pH changed from 1.81 to 7.0, and slightly increased in the pH range of 7.0 to 9.18. Finally, the PL intensity of the CDs remained unchanged in the range of 9.18–11.98 (Fig. S5A, ESI†). The PL intensity varies reversibly while the pH was switched repeatedly between 2 and 12 (Fig. S5B, ESI†). The possible reason was that the protonation degree of carboxylate groups, namely, the surface charge, affected the luminescent efficiency of CDs. In the acidic condition, CDs tended to aggregate due to the hydrogen bonds of the surface groups, resulting in the quenching of their fluorescence. On the contrary, CDs could remain mono-dispersed under alkaline condition due to the repulsive forces of the negative charges generated from the deprotonating of hydroxyls and carboxyls.

The fluorescence of CDs could be quenched by the Fe3+. Firstly, we explored the feasibility of using the CDs for Fe3+ detection. Fig. 3A showed that the fluorescence of CDs decreased gradually when the concentrations of Fe3+ increased from 0.02μmol/L to 8μmol/L. A linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (1−F/F 0) and the concentration of Fe3+ from 0.02μmol/L to 0.6μmol/L was found (Inset of Fig.3A), where F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensities at 400nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+ was that hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs could highly interact with Fe3+ because of a low solubility product constant (K sp) of metal hydroxides (the K sp of Fe(OH)3 was 4.0×10−38). The CDs would aggregate with each other in the presence of Fe3+, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of CDs [34].

To evaluate the selectivity of the detection of Fe3+ of CDs, we examined the fluorescence change of the CDs in the presence of other representative metal ions under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3B, K+, Na+, and Ca2+, whose content were higher than that of Fe3+, had not interfere the detection of Fe3+. Some metal ions, such as Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, and Co2+ could quench the fluorescence of CDs to some extent, however, they would not interfere the detection of intracellular Fe3+ due to the presence of trace amount in cells. Therefore, the CDs were promising candidates for the selective detection of Fe3+ in cells.

Considering the biomedical applications of CDs, we firstly evaluated the cytotoxicity of the CDs via the CKK-8 assay with A549 cells as a target. As expected, the CDs exhibited a good biocompatibility even the concentration of CDs up to 40μg/mL (Fig. S6, ESI†).

Because of various advantages of CDs, including small size, photostability, and good biocompatibility, we further investigated the intracellular imaging of Fe3+ by using the as-prepared CDs and the result were displayed in Fig. 3C. Compared with the cell themselves, the cell incubated with CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, suggesting that CDs could be transported into the living cells via endocytosis and reside in cytoplasm instead of nucleus. After the addition of 0.02mmol/L of Fe3+ in the incubation medium, the bright blue fluorescence of cells became dimmer and almost quenched followed by the further incubation with 0.1mmol/L of Fe3+, which implied that the CDs could achieve the sensitive and selective bioimaging of Fe3+.

In summary, we have developed a simple and efficient SSS method to prepare blue luminescent CDs with a QY of 4.82% from C60 and successfully achieved the surface carboxylation of CDs. The as-obtained fluorescent surface carboxylated CDs possess many good properties, such as uniform size distribution, photostability, salt tolerance, and biocompatibility. Notably, we applied the as-prepared fluorescent CDs into the selective and sensitive intracellular imaging of Fe3+ based on the fluorescence quenching of CDs. What is more, the CDs showed a pH-dependent photoluminescence, which suggested the further application of CDs in pH sensing of various environments.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21035005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.05.071.
